NoLiMa: Long-Context Evaluation Beyond Literal Matching

LLM   Ali Modarressi, Hanieh Deilamsalehy, Franck Dernoncourt, Trung Bui, Ryan A. Rossi et al.  ·  2025-02  ·  arXiv ↗
long-contextbenchmarkNIAHattention-mechanismRoPEevaluation
Summary
Ali Modarressi, Hanieh Deilamsalehy, Franck Dernoncourt, Trung Bui, Ryan A. Rossi, Seunghyun Yoon, Hinrich Schütze | 2025-02 | https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.05167
Category: llm | Tags: long-context, benchmark, NIAH, attention-mechanism, RoPE, evaluation, latent-reasoning
Read: 2026-04-19

Core Contribution

NoLiMa is a needle-in-a-haystack benchmark that strips away all literal/lexical overlap between the question and the relevant needle (ROUGE-L precision drops from ~0.86 in vanilla NIAH to ~0.07), forcing models to rely on latent associative reasoning — and on it the "128K-1M context" claims of every popular LLM collapse to effective lengths ≤ 8K tokens.

Summary

Long-context LLMs (GPT-4o, Gemini 1.5 Pro, Llama 3.x, Claude 3.5, Mistral Large 2, Jamba 1.5, Command R+, etc.) are routinely evaluated with NIAH-style benchmarks where the question and the planted needle share keywords (e.g. *"What is the magic number?"* → *"The magic number is 7."*). Such literal matches act as cheap attention magnets — induction heads can lock onto them regardless of context length — and inflate scores until benchmarks saturate. The authors quantify this with ROUGE-L precision and show that every popular long-context dataset has 0.5–0.96 surface overlap, while NoLiMa is engineered to ~0.07.

Method. A *needle set* of 58 question-needle pairs is constructed where each needle (e.g. *"Actually, Yuki lives next to the Semper Opera House."*) and its matching question (*"Which character has been to Dresden?"*) share no surface tokens. The link from question keyword $W_q$ to needle keyword $W_n$ requires either world knowledge (Semper Opera ↔ Dresden) or commonsense (vegan ↔ cannot eat fish). 1-hop and 2-hop variants probe deeper KB traversal, and "default" vs "inverted" word order probes causal-attention asymmetry. Haystacks are built from snippets of 10 open-license books with a two-stage filter pipeline that (i) Contriever-prunes any haystack word semantically similar to a $W_q$, and (ii) loops Llama-3.3-70B over chunks to flag and remove text that could serve as an alternative answer. Each (question × context length) is tested with 26 placements × 5 haystacks → 7,540 tests per L.

Results. Across 13 frontier models claiming ≥128K context, the effective length (L at which accuracy still ≥ 85% of the short-context base score) is ≤ 8K for every single model — most are ≤ 2K, even though base scores are >90%. By 32K, 11/13 fall below 50% of base. GPT-4o is the strongest exception but still drops 99.3 → 69.7 between base and 32K. Performance gaps between default vs inverted ordering and 1-hop vs 2-hop widen with L, and an aligned "last-2K" placement protocol shows that the decline is *not* explained by RoPE relative-distance — it is the sheer attention load from irrelevant tokens. CoT prompting and reasoning models (GPT-o1, GPT-o3-mini, R1-Distill-70B) help but do not close the gap above 16K. Inserting one distracting sentence with $W_q$ (irrelevant to the answer) collapses GPT-4o's effective length from 8K to 1K.

Key Findings

  • Universal long-context regression: 13/13 frontier LLMs (GPT-4o, Gemini 1.5 Pro/2.0 Flash, Llama 3.1/3.3 8B-405B, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Mistral Large 2, Jamba 1.5 Mini, Command R+) have effective length ≤ 8K on NoLiMa. Most are 1-2K, a 64-128× shortfall vs claimed length.
  • Literal matching was doing most of the work: Replacing the latent-link question with a literal "*Where does Yuki live?*" (Direct setup, Table 6) keeps Llama-3.3-70B at 98.5% even at 32K. Adding multiple-choice options also restores accuracy to 87-93%. Both confirm that *retrieval-by-keyword* and *retrieval-by-meaning* are very different tasks for current LLMs.
  • 2-hop adds a multiplicative penalty: Llama 3.3 70B at 32K — 1-hop 56.2% / 2-hop 25.9%; GPT-4o — 79.8% / 57.4% (Tables 11, 12). The gap widens with L, indicating that latent reasoning steps compose multiplicatively with attention-length cost.
  • Inverted needle order exposes causal-attention asymmetry: $W_n$ before [CHAR] is harder than [CHAR] before $W_n$ — when the model attends from $W_q$ to $W_n$, it cannot continue forward to find the answer character under causal masking, so it must use weaker forward-from-CHAR signals.
  • Last-2K aligned-depth experiment isolates pure attention load: For RoPE models, holding the question→needle relative distance constant while varying L still produces a steady accuracy drop (Fig 3d). This rules out position-encoding decay as the dominant cause; the bottleneck is attention SNR over the irrelevant token mass.
  • CoT and "thinking" only help mildly: CoT lifts Llama 3.3 70B 2-hop @ 32K from 25.9 → 34.3 (+8.4 pp absolute, +32% relative) but still far below the base. GPT-o1 on NoLiMa-Hard @ 32K reaches only 31.1%; GPT-o3-mini 18.9%; R1-Distill-70B 20.7%.
  • Distracting literal matches are catastrophic: A single neutral sentence "There was an article about Dresden in the daily newspaper." (containing $W_q$ but unrelated to the answer) collapses GPT-4o's effective length from 8K to 1K (Fig 5).
  • Updated Appendix E (v3): GPT-4.1 / Llama 4 / Gemini 2.5 Flash / Gemma 3 show the same pattern. GPT-4.1 has effective length 16K (best) but still drops below 65% at 128K despite a claimed 1M context.

Key Figures

Figure 1: Haystack filtering pipeline

What it shows: The semi-automatic loop that scrubs each haystack book of (a) words semantically similar to any question keyword $W_q$ and (b) text spans that could plausibly be interpreted as alternative answers.

Why it matters: Without this filter, the "no literal match" claim collapses — any leaked $W_q$ token in the haystack becomes a free attention magnet, and any plausible alternative answer would create false positives. The pipeline is what makes NoLiMa scores meaningful as an attention-load probe.

Detailed description: A book stream (left) is iteratively chunked; each chunk is paired with each needle question and fed (along with a few-shot prompt) to Llama 3.3 70B. The model returns either "N/A" or a candidate alternative-answer span. Flagged spans are manually reviewed by an author and either redacted or kept; the loop repeats until a full pass produces no further removals. The right side of the figure shows the same haystack with offending sentences highlighted in red and removed in the next iteration.

Figure 2(a): One-hop vs Two-hop normalized performance

What it shows: Normalized accuracy (accuracy / base score) of GPT-4o (purple) and Llama 3.3 70B (green) on 1-hop (solid) vs 2-hop (dashed) needles, x-axis = context length 1K-32K. Red dotted line at 0.85 = effective threshold.

Why it matters: Quantifies the cost of an extra latent reasoning step inside attention. The gap between solid and dashed lines is small at 1K but grows to 0.2-0.3 at 32K — long context does not just stress retrieval, it stresses reasoning *composition*.

Detailed description: All four lines start near 1.0 at 1K. GPT-4o 1-hop barely dips to ~0.85 at 32K; GPT-4o 2-hop drops to ~0.6. Llama 3.3 70B 1-hop and 2-hop both decline more steeply, with 2-hop falling fastest and ending near 0.3 at 32K.

Figure 2(b): Default vs Inverted needle order

What it shows: Same axes as 2(a), but solid = default needle order ([CHAR] then $W_n$), dashed = inverted ($W_n$ then [CHAR]).

Why it matters: The asymmetry is not random — it reveals that under causal masking, attention from a downstream token can resolve a latent link to an upstream entity (default), but the reverse (inverted) requires forward-from-CHAR backtracking that the model struggles with. This is direct evidence about *how* attention fails, not just *that* it fails.

Detailed description: Default lines stay above inverted at every L. The gap is ~5 pp at 1K, ~10-15 pp at 32K. Llama 3.3 70B inverted drops below 0.5 by 16K; GPT-4o inverted stays above 0.65 even at 32K.

Figure 3(b): Full-sweep depth plot (Two-hop, Llama 3.3 70B)

What it shows: Accuracy vs needle depth (0%=start, 100%=end) for context lengths 2K-32K (lighter→darker = longer L).

Why it matters: In vanilla NIAH the curves stay close to 1.0 at the endpoints and only dip in the middle ("lost-in-the-middle"). Here, larger L *uniformly depresses* the entire curve, including the endpoints. So the failure mode is not positional anymore — it is global attention dilution.

Detailed description: At 2K (lightest line), accuracy is ~0.8 across all depths. As L grows to 32K (darkest), the entire line shifts down to ~0.2-0.3, with no characteristic U-shape, just a flat low band.

Figure 3(d): Last-2K aligned sweep (Two-hop, Llama 3.3 70B)

What it shows: Same model and 2-hop test, but needles are now placed at the same physical token-distance from the END of the context across all L. X-axis = tokens-from-end (2000 → 0). Each line = a different L.

Why it matters: This is the cleanest disambiguation in the paper. RoPE relative distance is held constant for any fixed x-position, so position encoding cannot be the dominant cause of the L-dependent drop. The remaining variable is attention load — and we see lines clearly fan out as L grows.

Detailed description: For 2K context (lightest), accuracy hovers around 0.85. As L increases to 32K (darkest), the same physical depths now score 0.2-0.4. Crucially the curves are roughly *parallel* — same shape, different vertical offset — which is the signature of a global SNR effect rather than a positional bug.

Figure 4: Needle placement positions in Full Sweep vs Last-2K

What it shows: Diagram contrasting the two placement protocols. Top three rows (Full Sweep) show that placement positions scale proportionally with L — at 2K they cover the full strip, at 8K they are spread thinner. Bottom three rows (Last-2K) show placement positions clustered in the rightmost 2K tokens for every L; at 2K context the active region equals the haystack, at 8K it is the last quarter, etc.

Why it matters: Documents the experimental design that enables the position-vs-attention disentanglement in Figure 3(c,d). Without aligning the absolute token-distance from the question, you cannot separate RoPE decay from attention load.

Detailed description: Each strip shows blue (haystack) with red tick-marks for needle placements. Inactive haystack regions are grey. The Full Sweep maintains the same number of placements but spaces them by L; Last-2K maintains them at the same absolute positions from the end.

Figure 5: Distractor effect on length generalisation

What it shows: Normalized score vs L (1K-8K) for GPT-4o and Llama 3.3 70B, with (dashed) and without (solid) one inserted distractor sentence containing $W_q$.

Why it matters: The single most striking ablation in the paper. A neutral sentence with the question keyword (but irrelevant to the answer) is enough to collapse GPT-4o from "effective at 8K" to "effective at 1K". This is direct evidence that current LLMs prefer surface matches over latent meaning even when the surface match is content-empty — exactly the failure mode RAG systems hit when retrieved snippets share keywords with the query but lack the answer.

Detailed description: Solid lines (no distractor) stay near or above the 0.85 threshold up to 4K-8K. Dashed lines drop sharply: GPT-4o w/distractor crosses 0.85 between 1K and 2K; Llama 3.3 70B w/distractor is already below 0.85 at 1K and continues to fall, ending near 0.5 at 8K.

Key Tables

Table 1: ROUGE-L precision between question and context across benchmarks

BenchmarkR-1R-2R-L
∞Bench QA0.9660.5450.960
∞Bench MC0.9460.5060.932
RULER QA0.8090.4370.693
HELMET RAG0.6890.3040.555
Vanilla NIAH0.9050.7890.855
RULER S-NIAH0.5710.4610.500
BABILong (0K)0.5530.2380.522
NoLiMa0.0690.0020.067

Takeaway: Every popular long-context benchmark has a literal-overlap floor of at least 0.5 R-L. NoLiMa is one to two orders of magnitude lower — the only setup where models cannot exploit surface matching at all.

Table 3: NoLiMa main result — claimed vs effective length (subset)

ModelClaimedEffectiveBase1K4K8K16K32K
GPT-4o128K8K99.398.195.789.281.669.7
Llama 3.3 70B128K2K97.394.281.572.159.542.7
Llama 3.1 405B128K2K94.789.074.560.148.438.0
Gemini 1.5 Pro2M2K92.686.475.463.955.548.2
Gemini 2.0 Flash1M4K89.487.777.964.748.241.0
Claude 3.5 Sonnet200K4K87.585.477.661.745.729.8
Command R+128K<1K90.977.066.239.521.37.4
GPT-4o mini128K<1K84.867.744.232.620.613.7
Llama 3.1 8B128K1K76.765.744.131.922.614.2

Takeaway: Best-in-class effective length (8K) is 16× shorter than its claimed support (128K), and most models lose more than half their base accuracy by 32K. The "marketing" context length and the *useful* context length live in different orders of magnitude.

Table 6: Literal-match ablations (Llama 3.3 70B)

Setup8K16K32K
Direct (literal match question = vanilla NIAH)98.398.598.5
1-hop (NoLiMa)84.173.256.2
1-hop + Multiple Choice (literal-match options)98.797.493.1
2-hop (NoLiMa)57.442.725.9
2-hop + Multiple Choice96.394.687.2

Takeaway: Adding literal cues — either by rewording the question or by exposing the answer character names as MC options — almost completely restores long-context accuracy. The gap between NoLiMa (no cues) and NoLiMa-MC (cues) is the *price of literal matching*; it is enormous and roughly constant in absolute terms (~30-60 pp) across L.

Limitations

  • Synthetic, single-needle setup: The needle is a single short sentence about a unique character; real RAG / long-doc QA workloads have multiple relevant chunks, partial answers across chunks, and noisy retrieval. The benchmark stresses one specific failure mode (associative recall under no-overlap) but cannot predict how big that failure mode is in production.
  • Closed-source models are black boxes: For GPT/Gemini/Claude the authors can only treat models as oracles; no attention probing or ablations of the literal-vs-latent representation can be done, so the "attention dilution" mechanism remains a hypothesis grounded mainly in the open-weight Llama 3.3 70B experiments.
  • Filter LLM (Llama 3.3 70B) is itself fallible: The 99.8% control test only verifies it can spot conflicts when given the needle; it does not bound the rate of plausible-alternative-answer leaks remaining in the haystacks. Any residual leak would create noise in long-L scores.
  • No mechanistic interpretability: Authors explicitly note "deeper mechanistic analysis is beyond the scope of this paper." We see *that* causal attention struggles with inverted/2-hop in long contexts but not exactly *which* heads/layers fail. This leaves a clean follow-up: probe induction heads / function vectors as L grows on NoLiMa.
  • English-only, world-knowledge-anchored: Latent links use Western landmarks, dietary terms, etc. Multilingual or culture-shifted variants are not tested; non-English LLMs may fail for different reasons.
  • No training-side intervention: The paper diagnoses the disease but does not test the cure (e.g., does long-context training data with no literal matches help? Does YaRN/NTK/sliding-window architecture matter?). This is left to future work.

Infrastructure Impact

  • Algorithm: Forces a re-evaluation of long-context training. Training data that pairs queries with literal-overlap retrieval favors induction-head dominance; data that requires latent associative recall (RAG-style synthetic, paraphrased queries, knowledge-distillation traces) may be necessary to push effective length beyond ~10K. Also points to opportunities for *retrieval-aware decoding* — re-prompt the model with reformulated queries that surface the relevant entities.
  • Kernel: Modest direct impact. The diagnosis is "attention SNR" rather than a kernel-level inefficiency, but it weakens the case for sliding-window / sparse attention purely on quality grounds — the bottleneck is statistical, not computational.
  • Framework: Concrete operational warning for serving stacks (vLLM, SGLang, TRT-LLM): publishing a model's "supported context length" without a NoLiMa-style effective length disclaimer is misleading. Frameworks that auto-truncate or chunk long inputs should be tuned around the *effective* number, not the *claimed* number, especially for RAG pipelines where the relevant chunk has lexical drift from the query.
  • LLM: Architecture verdict — every standard causal Transformer (GQA Llama, MoE Jamba hybrid, Mistral Large 2, Llama 4 MoE in v3) shows the same pattern. Hybrid Mamba-Attention (Jamba) does *not* fix it, MoE does not fix it, model scale only modestly helps. Suggests a fundamental issue with softmax-attention-over-RoPE under low-SNR conditions, motivating research into *retrieval-augmented attention*, *learned sparsity*, or *fact-store-augmented* architectures (e.g., Memory Layers, Differentiable Search Indexes).
  • Agent: For agent runtimes that pile system prompts + tool docs + memory + observation history into one context window, NoLiMa says: tools the agent has not literally referenced recently are likely *invisible* even if they fit in the window. Agent designers must ensure recall-critical content (tool definitions, recent task state) lives in fresh, lexically-keyed positions or use external memory retrieval.
  • Code: Direct release at https://github.com/adobe-research/NoLiMa . Eval harness is plug-and-play with vLLM and OpenAI-style APIs; needle sets are JSON, haystacks pre-shuffled. Will likely become a standard regression suite alongside RULER and BABILong.
Details

Deep Analysis (llm)

Phase 2 deepening — constraint derivation, technical barrier, and ecosystem position

#### 时代定位 (Era Positioning)

NoLiMa lands at the moment when "1M / 10M context" has become a marketing arms race (Gemini 1.5 Pro 2M, Llama 4 Scout 10M, GPT-4.1 1M) and the original NIAH benchmark is *visibly* saturated — every flagship returns >95% on vanilla needle-in-a-haystack at multi-hundred-K context. The community already suspected that NIAH was too easy (RULER, HELMET, BABILong, ∞Bench all tried to harden it) but those harder variants increased *task complexity* (more needles, fact-chaining, arithmetic) rather than removing the *literal-match shortcut*. Going to "deep water" here means: stop testing keyword retrieval, start testing what the attention mechanism is supposed to do — *associate* meanings. NoLiMa is the first benchmark to make this distinction surgical (ROUGE-L precision 0.07 vs the 0.5+ floor of every prior work).

#### 背景 (Context — why prior NIAH variants don't suffice)

Prior NIAH extensions failed for two opposite reasons. (1) Some kept literal matches but added needles or distractor docs (RULER multi-NIAH, HELMET RAG) — the model still has a magnet to latch onto; harder, but the shortcut still exists. (2) Some removed literal matches but made the *task* itself harder regardless of context (BABILong counting at 0K = 28%; Ancestral Tree Challenge fails at <1K). When a benchmark is impossible at short context, you cannot tell whether long-context performance dropped *because of length* or *because of task difficulty*. NoLiMa's design constraint — "must be solvable at <1K with high accuracy" while "must have ROUGE precision near zero" — is the simultaneous satisfaction of two opposing constraints that nobody had achieved.

#### 约束推导 (Constraint Derivation — feasibility of the design space)

AlternativeWhy it fails as a "literal-match-free" long-context benchmark
Keep NIAH, hide the literal match by paraphrasingAuthor of question and author of needle still tend to use overlapping content words; ROUGE creeps back up. Hard to control to < 0.1.
Use translation across languagesConflates long-context evaluation with translation; opens up tokenization bias.
Synthetic latent-association graph (e.g. random fact triples)Models cannot infer the latent link unless the fact is in their parametric KB; benchmark would test memory of synthetic facts not attention.
Hand-curated world-knowledge needles with character placeholder✓ This is what NoLiMa does. Also enforces uniqueness of $W_n \to W_q$ link to avoid ambiguity.
Replace "character name" answer with arbitrary tokenLoses the elegant property that any test reduces to a string-contains check; opens up partial-credit headaches.

Why not just use longer "haystacks" of natural prose? Because natural prose has emergent literal overlaps; the filter pipeline (Section 3.1) is the mechanism that suppresses those.

#### 破局 (Insight — the "aha")

The authors' insight is that the attention mechanism's strongest feature — induction heads that copy from any literal match — is also its biggest evaluation hazard. As long as a benchmark contains *any* lexical bridge between query and answer location, you are testing induction heads, not associative recall. Strip the bridges, and what is left is "find the meaning in 60K tokens of irrelevant prose" — which is what every customer who builds a RAG pipeline actually wants.

Plain-language analogy: NIAH is like asking a visitor to find the apartment of "John Smith" by giving them the building directory. NoLiMa says: forget the directory, here's a 60K-word novel about that neighborhood, and the apartment is the one belonging to "the engineer who lives next to the famous opera house"; you must already know which opera house is in which city. The model's memory of world facts is fine — it's the ability to *use* that memory while filtering 60K tokens that breaks.

#### 核心技术壁垒 (Core Technical Barrier — what makes this benchmark itself hard to replicate)

The hardest replicable artifact is the two-stage haystack-filter pipeline, particularly Stage 2 (plausible-alternative-answer filtering). Stripping all lexical bridges to $W_q$ is mechanical (Contriever cosine + threshold + sentence removal), but ensuring there is no *semantic alternative answer* in 60K tokens of book prose requires running an LLM over every chunk × every question pair, manually reviewing each flag, and iterating until convergence. The 99.8% control-test score (when a needle is *intentionally* placed in a chunk, the filter LLM detects 99.8% of the time) is the precise calibration step that makes the resulting accuracy numbers attributable to the model under test rather than to leakage. A naive replication of NoLiMa that skips this step would silently inflate scores at long context (because models would find the leaked alternative answer and get partial credit).

#### 质疑假设 (Challenge Assumptions)

The paper assumes (i) the model has the world knowledge to make the latent link, (ii) the filter pipeline removes essentially all alternative answers, (iii) accuracy on a single-character contains-check is a good proxy for general long-context comprehension. (i) is well-supported by the high base scores. (ii) rests on a 99.8% control-test number that does not bound *false negatives* (chunks where an alternative answer exists but the filter missed it). (iii) is the weakest — a contains-check rewards the model for *guessing* the right name from the small character pool; in principle a model could ignore the haystack entirely and still pick a plausible name. The base scores at <1K are not 100% (most are 76-99%) which suggests some answers depend on careful reading rather than guessing, but a stricter exact-span metric or a "no-answer" distractor needle would tighten this. Personally I am also not 100% sure that the "distractor sentence with $W_q$" experiment (Fig 5) generalizes to *naturally occurring* lexical noise in a real RAG corpus — the authors' distractor sentence template is unnaturally specific ("There was an article about $W_q$ in the daily newspaper").

#### 设计绑定批判 (Design Binding Critique)

NoLiMa binds itself to (a) causal Transformer architectures with (b) softmax attention and (c) English world knowledge. If a model uses non-causal, sliding-window, or routing-based attention, the inverted-vs-default and last-2K analyses change meaning (e.g., a fully bidirectional encoder would erase the inversion gap). If a model has *external* retrieval (RAG, function calling) it could short-circuit the latent link entirely — NoLiMa is a benchmark of *internal* attention, not of any model's full pipeline. And the world-knowledge anchoring means models heavy on Western training data look better than they would on culture-shifted variants.

#### 实践上下文 (Deployment Context)

NoLiMa's findings apply most directly to: long-document QA over heterogeneous sources, RAG with low BM25 overlap (i.e. when the gold passage is paraphrased relative to the query), agent runtimes with bloated context (system prompt + tool catalogue + scratch + observations), and ICL with many-shot demonstrations spread over thousands of tokens. They apply *less* to: code-completion (queries usually overlap the codebase keywords), summarisation (the entire document is "relevant"), and translation (no recall needed).

#### 生态影响追踪 (Ecosystem Influence)

Within months of release, NoLiMa was cited by Llama 4 (Maverick/Scout) and Gemini 2.5 Flash papers; OpenAI mentioned similar evals in the GPT-4.1 blog (which improved long-context scores notably). The Adobe-Research GitHub repo (~stars climbing) is being added to OpenCompass and lm-eval-harness. The follow-on NoLiMa-Hard subset (10 hardest needles) is now the default benchmark to compare reasoning models — GPT-o3 currently leads at 58.5% / 32K. Conceptually, NoLiMa has shifted the "long-context" conversation from "claimed length" to "effective length on associative recall" — RULER's effective-length methodology is now standard.

---

1. Architecture & Implementation Cross-Reference

#### 1a. NoLiMa is a benchmark, not a model — but the test harness is a real artifact

NoLiMa does not introduce a new model architecture; it instruments existing LLMs. The "implementation cross-reference" therefore targets the eval harness at https://github.com/adobe-research/NoLiMa, not a modeling_xxx.py. We cloned the repo and walked through data/book_haystack.py and evaluation/run_tests.py (paper does not even document the inner workings of BookHaystack — code is the only ground truth).

Key implementation facts that the paper underspecifies:

  • Insertion-position resolution: BookHaystack requires a \n token at the chosen depth, not an arbitrary token boundary. It tokenises the book, records every position whose decoded token contains \n, and inserts at the closest such position to int(round(L · depth)). So "needle at depth 50%" means "at the closest newline boundary on or after token 0.5L". This avoids splitting sentences but biases placement to wherever paragraph breaks happen.
  • Encoding/decoding callbacks (encoding_func, decoding_func) are model-specific — the same depth percentage produces *different absolute byte positions* across tokenisers, which is why the authors report token-level depth and use "static_depth" mode to align across models when needed.
  • Distractor placement (Section 4.4.4) is implemented in generate_w_needle_placement with distractor_free_zone=0.2 — at minimum 20% of the context length must separate distractor and needle, and the distractor is restricted to the 20%-80% middle region. This is the operationalisation of "the distractor has no proximity advantage".
  • Engine support (evaluation/async_api_connector.py): vLLM, OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, AWS Bedrock, and now generic OpenRouter-style endpoints. Greedy decoding for instruction models; default sampling for o1/o3-mini; top_p=0.95, temp=0.6 for R1-Distill.
  • Scoring: metric: contains — a test passes iff the gold character name appears as a substring in the model's output. Case-insensitive. No partial credit, no per-test confidence.

#### 1b. Architecture diagram

NoLiMa's "architecture" is the conceptual flow of a single test, not a neural network. The diagram below captures (1) the task decomposition into haystack/question/latent-link, (2) the filtering pipeline, (3) needle variants and the causal-attention failure mechanism, and (4) the eval harness with placement protocols.

width="380" height="900" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="haystack_lbl" value="Haystack &#xa;(60K+ tokens, randomly concatenated &#xa;snippets from 10 open-license books, &#xa;each snippet &lt; 250 tokens)" style="text;align=center;fontSize=12;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="50" y="70" width="360" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="hay_top" value="...irrelevant book text...&#xa;...irrelevant book text...&#xa;...irrelevant book text..." style="rounded=0;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#eeeeee;strokeColor=#999999;fontSize=10;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="60" y="160" width="340" height="200" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- The needle --> <mxCell id="needle_box" value="NEEDLE (1 sentence)&#xa;&#xa;Actually, [CHAR=Yuki] lives next&#xa;to the W_n = Semper Opera House.&#xa;&#xa;(placed at depth d, controlled)" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#fff2cc;strokeColor=#d6b656;fontSize=11;fontStyle=1;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="60" y="380" width="340" height="120" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="hay_bot" value="...irrelevant book text...&#xa;...irrelevant book text...&#xa;(filtered: no W_q, no answer leak)" style="rounded=0;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#eeeeee;strokeColor=#999999;fontSize=10;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="60" y="520" width="340" height="420" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- Question column --> <mxCell id="q_lbl" value="Question (W_q in question)" style="text;align=center;fontSize=12;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="480" y="70" width="320" height="30" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="q_one" value="ONE-HOP&#xa;&#xa;W_q = &quot;Dresden&quot;&#xa;&#xa;&quot;Which character has been&#xa;to Dresden?&quot;" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#dae8fc;strokeColor=#6c8ebf;fontSize=11;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="480" y="120" width="320" height="120" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="q_two" value="TWO-HOP&#xa;&#xa;W_q = &quot;Saxony&quot; (state)&#xa;&#xa;&quot;Which character has been&#xa;to the state of Saxony?&quot;" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#dae8fc;strokeColor=#6c8ebf;fontSize=11;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="480" y="270" width="320" height="120" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="q_constr" value="Critical constraint:&#xa;ROUGE-L precision(question, needle) ≈ 0.067&#xa;(vanilla NIAH ≈ 0.855)&#xa;⇒ NO literal/lexical overlap, only semantic link" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#f8cecc;strokeColor=#b85450;fontSize=10;align=left;fontStyle=2;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="480" y="420" width="320" height="100" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- Latent association graph --> <mxCell id="assoc_box" value="" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#f5f5f5;strokeColor=#666666;dashed=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="860" y="60" width="800" height="500" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="assoc_lbl" value="Latent association (lives in model's parametric world knowledge)" style="text;align=center;fontSize=12;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="870" y="70" width="780" height="30" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="char" value="CHAR&#xa;&quot;Yuki&quot;&#xa;(answer)" style="ellipse;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#d4edda;strokeColor=#28a745;fontSize=12;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="900" y="200" width="140" height="100" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="wn" value="W_n&#xa;&quot;Semper Opera House&quot;&#xa;(needle keyword)" style="ellipse;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#fff2cc;strokeColor=#d6b656;fontSize=11;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="1120" y="200" width="160" height="100" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="wq1" value="W_q (1-hop)&#xa;&quot;Dresden&quot;&#xa;(city)" style="ellipse;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#dae8fc;strokeColor=#6c8ebf;fontSize=11;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="1360" y="120" width="160" height="100" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="wq2" value="W_q (2-hop)&#xa;&quot;Saxony&quot;&#xa;(state)" style="ellipse;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#dae8fc;strokeColor=#6c8ebf;fontSize=11;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="1360" y="280" width="160" height="100" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- Edges --> <mxCell id="e1" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;exitX=1;exitY=0.5;entryX=0;entryY=0.5;strokeColor=#666666;" edge="1" parent="1" source="char" target="wn"> <mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="e1_lbl" value="explicitly stated&#xa;in needle text" style="text;fontSize=10;align=center;fontStyle=2;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="1040" y="180" width="100" height="30" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="e2" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;exitX=1;exitY=0.3;entryX=0;entryY=0.5;strokeColor=#b85450;dashed=1;" edge="1" parent="1" source="wn" target="wq1"> <mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="e2_lbl" value="latent hop 1&#xa;(world knowledge:&#xa;opera is in Dresden)" style="text;fontSize=10;align=center;fontStyle=2;fontColor=#b85450;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="1280" y="120" width="120" height="50" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="e3" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;exitX=1;exitY=0.7;entryX=0;entryY=0.5;strokeColor=#b85450;dashed=1;" edge="1" parent="1" source="wn" target="wq2"> <mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="e3_lbl" value="latent hop 1+2&#xa;(opera→Dresden→Saxony)" style="text;fontSize=10;align=center;fontStyle=2;fontColor=#b85450;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="1280" y="350" width="120" height="40" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- Causal attention narrative --> <mxCell id="attn_box" value="What the LLM has to do (causal attention only):&#xa;1. From query token (W_q in question), attend back to SOME token in haystack carrying W_n.&#xa;2. Resolve the latent link W_q ↔ W_n via parametric knowledge — there is NO surface match to lock onto.&#xa;3. From W_n, backtrace to the [CHAR] token mentioned earlier in the same needle sentence.&#xa;Failure mode: at long context, attention probability over the relevant W_n token is diluted; without literal matches as &#xa;a &quot;magnet&quot;, the relevance signal drops below noise floor of 60K+ irrelevant tokens." style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#ffe6cc;strokeColor=#d79b00;fontSize=11;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="880" y="600" width="780" height="180" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="results_box" value="Headline results (Table 3):&#xa;• 13/13 models with claimed context ≥ 128K have effective length ≤ 8K on NoLiMa (most ≤ 2K).&#xa;• 11/13 fall below 50% of base score by 32K. Even GPT-4o drops 99.3% → 69.7% at 32K.&#xa;• Adding ONE distractor sentence containing W_q (irrelevant) collapses GPT-4o eff. length: 8K → 1K." style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#f8cecc;strokeColor=#b85450;fontSize=11;align=left;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="880" y="800" width="780" height="140" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> </root> </mxGraphModel> </diagram> <!-- ============================================================== --> <!-- Page 2: Filtering pipeline (haystack construction) --> <!-- ============================================================== --> <diagram id="page2" name="2. Haystack Filtering Pipeline"> <mxGraphModel dx="1400" dy="900" grid="1" gridSize="10" guides="1" tooltips="1" connect="1" arrows="1" fold="1" page="1" pageScale="1" pageWidth="1700" pageHeight="900" math="0" shadow="0"> <root> <mxCell id="0"/> <mxCell id="1" parent="0"/> <mxCell id="title" value="NoLiMa Haystack Filtering Pipeline (Section 3.1) — eliminate (1) lexical/semantic distractors of W_q and (2) text spans that could serve as plausible alternative answers" style="text;align=left;fontSize=13;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="20" y="10" width="1660" height="30" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- Stage 1: distractor filter --> <mxCell id="s1_box" value="" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#fff5f5;strokeColor=#b85450;dashed=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="40" y="60" width="780" height="380" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="s1_lbl" value="Stage 1: Distractor Word Filtering&#xa;(per-question keyword W_q)" style="text;align=center;fontSize=12;fontStyle=1;fontColor=#b85450;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="50" y="70" width="760" height="30" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="b1" value="10 open-license books&#xa;each ≥ 50K tokens" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#fff2cc;strokeColor=#d6b656;fontSize=11;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="60" y="120" width="200" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="b2" value="Embed every word with&#xa;Contriever (Izacard 2022)" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#dae8fc;strokeColor=#6c8ebf;fontSize=11;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="300" y="120" width="220" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="b3" value="Top-20 by dot-product&#xa;similarity to each W_q" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#dae8fc;strokeColor=#6c8ebf;fontSize=11;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="560" y="120" width="220" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="b4" value="Manual review by author —&#xa;flag words with substring match&#xa;or high semantic match to W_q" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#e1d5e7;strokeColor=#9673a6;fontSize=11;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="60" y="220" width="280" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="b5" value="Remove ALL sentences&#xa;containing flagged words" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#f8cecc;strokeColor=#b85450;fontSize=11;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="380" y="220" width="200" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="b6" value="Distractor-clean&#xa;haystack pool" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#d4edda;strokeColor=#28a745;fontSize=11;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="620" y="220" width="160" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="s1_note" value="Why this matters: any haystack token sharing surface form with W_q would &#xa;give the model a free attention magnet, defeating the &quot;no literal match&quot; design.&#xa;Section 4.4.4 measures this effect explicitly." style="text;fontSize=10;align=left;fontStyle=2;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="60" y="310" width="720" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- arrows stage 1 --> <mxCell id="se1" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;" edge="1" parent="1" source="b1" target="b2"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <mxCell id="se2" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;" edge="1" parent="1" source="b2" target="b3"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <mxCell id="se3" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;exitX=0.5;exitY=1;entryX=0.5;entryY=0;" edge="1" parent="1" source="b3" target="b4"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <mxCell id="se4" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;" edge="1" parent="1" source="b4" target="b5"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <mxCell id="se5" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;" edge="1" parent="1" source="b5" target="b6"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <!-- Stage 2: answer leak filter --> <mxCell id="s2_box" value="" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#fff8e1;strokeColor=#d79b00;dashed=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="860" y="60" width="800" height="780" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="s2_lbl" value="Stage 2: Plausible-Alternative-Answer Filtering (semi-automatic loop)" style="text;align=center;fontSize=12;fontStyle=1;fontColor=#d79b00;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="870" y="70" width="780" height="30" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="c1" value="Distractor-clean haystack" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#d4edda;strokeColor=#28a745;fontSize=11;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="900" y="120" width="200" height="50" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="c2" value="Sliding window&#xa;chunk size 1000 chars (~250 tok)&#xa;stride 800 chars" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#dae8fc;strokeColor=#6c8ebf;fontSize=11;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="1180" y="120" width="240" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="c3" value="For each (chunk, question) pair:&#xa;prompt Llama 3.3 70B-Instruct&#xa;with few-shot examples" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#e1d5e7;strokeColor=#9673a6;fontSize=11;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="900" y="220" width="320" height="70" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="c4" value="Output:&#xa;&quot;N/A&quot; OR candidate-answer span" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#fff2cc;strokeColor=#d6b656;fontSize=11;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="1280" y="220" width="320" height="70" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="c5" value="Author manually reviews&#xa;every flagged span;&#xa;decides remove vs keep" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#f8cecc;strokeColor=#b85450;fontSize=11;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="1080" y="320" width="320" height="70" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="c6" value="Loop until &quot;no further removals&quot;" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#ffe6cc;strokeColor=#d79b00;fontSize=11;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="1080" y="430" width="320" height="50" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="c7" value="Final book = answer-leak-free,&#xa;distractor-free, ready for haystack assembly" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#d4edda;strokeColor=#28a745;fontSize=11;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="1080" y="510" width="320" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- arrows stage 2 --> <mxCell id="ce1" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;" edge="1" parent="1" source="c1" target="c2"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <mxCell id="ce2" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;exitX=0.5;exitY=1;entryX=0.5;entryY=0;" edge="1" parent="1" source="c2" target="c4"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <mxCell id="ce3" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;exitX=0.5;exitY=1;entryX=0.5;entryY=0;" edge="1" parent="1" source="c1" target="c3"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <mxCell id="ce4" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;exitX=0.5;exitY=1;entryX=0.5;entryY=0;" edge="1" parent="1" source="c3" target="c5"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <mxCell id="ce5" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;exitX=0.5;exitY=1;entryX=0.5;entryY=0;" edge="1" parent="1" source="c4" target="c5"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <mxCell id="ce6" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;" edge="1" parent="1" source="c5" target="c6"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <mxCell id="ce7" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;" edge="1" parent="1" source="c6" target="c7"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <mxCell id="ce8" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;dashed=1;exitX=0;exitY=0.5;entryX=0;entryY=0.5;" edge="1" parent="1" source="c6" target="c1"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <mxCell id="ce8_lbl" value="re-scan&#xa;cleaned text" style="text;fontSize=10;fontStyle=2;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="900" y="380" width="80" height="30" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- Validation --> <mxCell id="val_box" value="" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#e8f5e9;strokeColor=#28a745;dashed=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="900" y="600" width="720" height="220" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="val_lbl" value="Filter validity check" style="text;align=center;fontSize=12;fontStyle=1;fontColor=#28a745;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="910" y="610" width="700" height="30" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="v1" value="Insert each needle into 100 random chunks. Ask Llama 3.3 70B&#xa;the question. Check whether it can identify the conflict.&#xa;&#xa;Result: 99.8% accuracy ⇒ filter LLM understands the task &amp;&#xa;has the world knowledge to spot a true alternative answer." style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#d4edda;strokeColor=#28a745;fontSize=11;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="930" y="650" width="660" height="140" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- Final assembly note --> <mxCell id="final_note" value="Haystack assembly (Section 4.1):&#xa;• Iteratively pick a random book &amp; extract a continuous snippet &lt; 250 tokens, append until total ≥ 2K lines (≥ 60K tokens). &#xa;• Memorisation defence: snippet-shuffling breaks any large contiguous passage the model could recall.&#xa;• Per context length L, place each needle 26 times at equal-spaced depths × 5 random haystacks × 58 needle-question pairs = 7,540 tests." style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#fff2cc;strokeColor=#d6b656;fontSize=11;align=left;fontStyle=2;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="40" y="500" width="780" height="160" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> </root> </mxGraphModel> </diagram> <!-- ============================================================== --> <!-- Page 3: Default vs Inverted needle order; 1-hop vs 2-hop --> <!-- ============================================================== --> <diagram id="page3" name="3. Needle Variants &amp; Causal-Attention Failure"> <mxGraphModel dx="1400" dy="900" grid="1" gridSize="10" guides="1" tooltips="1" connect="1" arrows="1" fold="1" page="1" pageScale="1" pageWidth="1700" pageHeight="1100" math="0" shadow="0"> <root> <mxCell id="0"/> <mxCell id="1" parent="0"/> <mxCell id="title" value="Why Inverted &amp; Two-hop Needles Are Harder — a causal-attention story" style="text;align=left;fontSize=14;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="20" y="10" width="1660" height="30" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- Default panel --> <mxCell id="def_box" value="" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#e8f5e9;strokeColor=#28a745;dashed=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="40" y="60" width="800" height="450" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="def_lbl" value="Default order: &quot;... [CHAR=Yuki] lives next to the W_n=Semper Opera House.&quot;" style="text;align=center;fontSize=12;fontStyle=1;fontColor=#28a745;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="50" y="70" width="780" height="30" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- token strip --> <mxCell id="d_chr" value="[CHAR]&#xa;Yuki" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#d4edda;strokeColor=#28a745;fontSize=11;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="80" y="120" width="100" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="d_pred" value="lives next to the" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#eeeeee;strokeColor=#999999;fontSize=10;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="200" y="120" width="200" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="d_wn" value="W_n&#xa;Semper Opera House" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#fff2cc;strokeColor=#d6b656;fontSize=11;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="420" y="120" width="200" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="d_haystack" value="...60K-token haystack...&#xa;Question prompt at end:&#xa;&quot;Which char has been to W_q=Dresden?&quot;" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#dae8fc;strokeColor=#6c8ebf;fontSize=11;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="640" y="120" width="180" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- causal attention path --> <mxCell id="d_path" value="Causal attention path (right→left):&#xa;1. W_q=&quot;Dresden&quot; in query attends back &amp; finds W_n=&quot;Semper Opera House&quot;&#xa; via latent association (Dresden ↔ opera in parametric KB)&#xa;2. From W_n token, attend back to nearby [CHAR] token in same needle sentence&#xa;3. Output [CHAR] = &quot;Yuki&quot;&#xa;⇒ TWO causal hops, both well-supported — works up to large L." style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#fff2cc;strokeColor=#d6b656;fontSize=11;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="80" y="220" width="740" height="160" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="d_score" value="Empirical (Fig 2b, Llama 3.3 70B, 32K): default ≈ 0.74&#xa;GPT-4o default ≈ 0.81 — both above inverted by ~10 pp" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#d4edda;strokeColor=#28a745;fontSize=11;align=left;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="80" y="400" width="740" height="80" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- Inverted panel --> <mxCell id="inv_box" value="" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#fff5f5;strokeColor=#b85450;dashed=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="870" y="60" width="800" height="450" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="inv_lbl" value="Inverted order: &quot;W_n=Semper Opera House is next to where [CHAR=Yuki] lives.&quot;" style="text;align=center;fontSize=12;fontStyle=1;fontColor=#b85450;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="880" y="70" width="780" height="30" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="i_wn" value="W_n&#xa;Semper Opera House" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#fff2cc;strokeColor=#d6b656;fontSize=11;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="900" y="120" width="200" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="i_pred" value="is next to where" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#eeeeee;strokeColor=#999999;fontSize=10;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="1120" y="120" width="200" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="i_chr" value="[CHAR]&#xa;Yuki" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#d4edda;strokeColor=#28a745;fontSize=11;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="1340" y="120" width="100" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="i_haystack" value="...60K-token haystack...&#xa;Question:&#xa;&quot;Which char has been to W_q=Dresden?&quot;" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#dae8fc;strokeColor=#6c8ebf;fontSize=11;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="1460" y="120" width="180" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="i_path" value="Causal attention path (right→left):&#xa;1. W_q=&quot;Dresden&quot; in query attends back to W_n=&quot;Semper Opera House&quot; — OK (latent link).&#xa;2. From W_n token, look BACKWARD for an answer ⇒ but [CHAR] is to the RIGHT&#xa; of W_n in the sentence. The W_n token cannot see [CHAR] under causal masking.&#xa;3. Model must instead attend FORWARD-from-[CHAR] to recall its predecessors —&#xa; this is essentially the reverse of induction-head behaviour and is&#xa; much weaker, especially at long L." style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#f8cecc;strokeColor=#b85450;fontSize=11;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="900" y="220" width="740" height="160" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="i_score" value="Empirical (Fig 2b, 32K): inverted ≈ 0.51 (Llama 3.3 70B), 0.66 (GPT-4o)&#xa;⇒ Performance gap to default widens with L — confirms causal-attention bottleneck." style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#f8cecc;strokeColor=#b85450;fontSize=11;align=left;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="900" y="400" width="740" height="80" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- 1-hop vs 2-hop --> <mxCell id="hop_title" value="One-hop vs Two-hop reasoning load" style="text;align=left;fontSize=13;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="40" y="540" width="900" height="30" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="oh_box" value="ONE-HOP&#xa;&#xa;W_q=&quot;Dresden&quot;&#xa;Latent link: &quot;Semper Opera House&quot; ↔ &quot;Dresden&quot;&#xa;⇒ single fact retrieval from KB at the same time as recall.&#xa;&#xa;Llama 3.3 70B (32K, no CoT, Tab 11): 56.2%&#xa;GPT-4o (32K): 79.8%" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#dae8fc;strokeColor=#6c8ebf;fontSize=11;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="40" y="580" width="800" height="220" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="th_box" value="TWO-HOP&#xa;&#xa;W_q=&quot;Saxony&quot;&#xa;Latent chain: &quot;Semper Opera House&quot; → &quot;Dresden&quot; → &quot;Saxony&quot;&#xa;⇒ multi-step KB traversal happening *inside* attention&#xa;⇒ weaker per-step probability ⇒ noise floor reached earlier.&#xa;&#xa;Llama 3.3 70B (32K, no CoT, Tab 12): 25.9%&#xa;GPT-4o (32K): 57.4%&#xa;&#xa;Effect (Fig 2a): 1-hop vs 2-hop gap WIDENS as L grows.&#xa;Effect (Fig 3b): full-sweep performance distribution flattens DOWN to&#xa;~0 at every depth, not just middle. Lost-in-the-middle disappears&#xa;because the whole curve drops." style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#f8cecc;strokeColor=#b85450;fontSize=11;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="870" y="580" width="800" height="350" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> </root> </mxGraphModel> </diagram> <!-- ============================================================== --> <!-- Page 4: Evaluation harness + Placement protocols --> <!-- ============================================================== --> <diagram id="page4" name="4. Eval Harness &amp; Placement Protocols"> <mxGraphModel dx="1400" dy="900" grid="1" gridSize="10" guides="1" tooltips="1" connect="1" arrows="1" fold="1" page="1" pageScale="1" pageWidth="1700" pageHeight="950" math="0" shadow="0"> <root> <mxCell id="0"/> <mxCell id="1" parent="0"/> <mxCell id="title" value="NoLiMa Evaluation Harness — placement, scoring, and the &quot;effective length&quot; metric" style="text;align=left;fontSize=14;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="20" y="10" width="1660" height="30" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- Pipeline --> <mxCell id="ip" value="Inputs:&#xa;• needle_set.json (58 q-needle pairs)&#xa;• haystack/rand_shuffle/*.txt (10 books, distractor- &amp; answer-leak-filtered)&#xa;• model_config.json (engine: vllm | openai | gemini | anthropic | bedrock)" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#dae8fc;strokeColor=#6c8ebf;fontSize=11;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="40" y="60" width="500" height="100" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="bh" value="BookHaystack.generate_w_needle_placement(...)&#xa;• Encode book to tokens; record positions where token contains '\n' as valid insert points.&#xa;• For target context_length L and depth d ∈ [0, 1]:&#xa; start = nearest_newline_before(d·L)&#xa; end = start + L&#xa;• Insert needle at depth d, optionally insert distractor sentence.&#xa;• Returns dict{ text, static_depth, token_depth, depth, context_length_wo_needle }" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#fff2cc;strokeColor=#d6b656;fontSize=11;align=left;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="600" y="60" width="780" height="150" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="run" value="run_tests.py × 8 context lengths {250, 500, 1K, 2K, 4K, 8K, 16K, 32K}&#xa;For each L: 26 placements × 5 haystacks × 58 q-needle pairs = 7,540 tests" style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#e1d5e7;strokeColor=#9673a6;fontSize=11;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="40" y="200" width="1340" height="60" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="prompt" value="Task prompt (Appendix C):&#xa;&quot;You will answer a question based on the following book snippet:&#xa;{haystack with embedded needle}&#xa;Use the information provided in the book snippet to answer the question. Your answer should be short and based on either&#xa;explicitly stated facts or strong, logical inferences.&#xa;Question: {question}&#xa;Return only the final answer with no additional explanation or reasoning.&quot;&#xa;CoT variant: ask for ≤ 3 sentences of reasoning, then final answer on a new line. Hard cap 192 generated tokens.&#xa;Reasoning models (o1/o3/R1): up to 1536 tokens incl. reasoning trace." style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#fff2cc;strokeColor=#d6b656;fontSize=11;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="40" y="290" width="800" height="220" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="metric" value="Scoring (metric=&quot;contains&quot;):&#xa;• Test passes iff generated answer string contains the gold character name.&#xa;• Per (q, needle, L): average across 5 haystacks.&#xa;• Per L: average across 58 q-needle pairs ⇒ accuracy(L).&#xa;&#xa;Base score:&#xa;• For each (q, needle): take MAX over L ∈ {250, 500, 1K} of mean accuracy.&#xa;• Base score = average across all (q, needle).&#xa;• Threshold = 0.85 × base score.&#xa;&#xa;Effective length:&#xa;• max L such that accuracy(L) ≥ 0.85 × base.&#xa;• Normalised score plots: accuracy(L) / base." style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#d4edda;strokeColor=#28a745;fontSize=11;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="880" y="290" width="500" height="320" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- Placement protocols --> <mxCell id="proto_lbl" value="Two placement protocols for depth analysis" style="text;align=left;fontSize=13;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="40" y="540" width="800" height="30" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="full_box" value="Full Sweep (the classic NIAH plot)&#xa;• Place needle at depths 0%, 4%, 8%, … 100% of L.&#xa;• Plot accuracy vs depth, separate line per L.&#xa;• Reveals &quot;lost-in-the-middle&quot; (Liu 2024) when applicable.&#xa;• Confound: at large L, the same depth % is a much&#xa; bigger absolute distance from the question token.&#xa; Cannot disentangle position-encoding from attention-load." style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#dae8fc;strokeColor=#6c8ebf;fontSize=11;align=left;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="40" y="580" width="800" height="200" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <mxCell id="last2k_box" value="Last-2K Sweep (NoLiMa novelty, Section 4.4.2)&#xa;• Align needle placements within the LAST 2K tokens of every context length.&#xa;• X-axis: tokens-from-end (0 to 2000). Same physical x-position across all L.&#xa;• Same RoPE relative distance between question &amp; needle for fixed x-position.&#xa;• Therefore, position-encoding decay is HELD CONSTANT.&#xa;• Any drop with growing L must be attributed to the volume of irrelevant&#xa; tokens the attention has to suppress.&#xa;• Result (Fig 3d, two-hop): plotlines drop nearly uniformly as L grows, even at the same&#xa; tokens-from-end ⇒ pure attention-overload signature, not a positional bug.&#xa;⇒ This is the cleanest experiment that ISOLATES &quot;long-context attention is&#xa; fundamentally weaker than short-context attention&quot; from confounders." style="rounded=1;whiteSpace=wrap;fillColor=#fff2cc;strokeColor=#d6b656;fontSize=11;align=left;fontStyle=1;" vertex="1" parent="1"> <mxGeometry x="870" y="580" width="800" height="290" as="geometry"/> </mxCell> <!-- arrows --> <mxCell id="ae1" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;" edge="1" parent="1" source="ip" target="bh"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <mxCell id="ae2" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;exitX=0.5;exitY=1;entryX=0.5;entryY=0;" edge="1" parent="1" source="bh" target="run"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <mxCell id="ae3" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;exitX=0.5;exitY=1;entryX=0.5;entryY=0;" edge="1" parent="1" source="run" target="prompt"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> <mxCell id="ae4" style="endArrow=classic;html=1;exitX=1;exitY=0.5;entryX=0;entryY=0.5;" edge="1" parent="1" source="prompt" target="metric"><mxGeometry relative="1" as="geometry"/></mxCell> </root> </mxGraphModel> </diagram> </mxfile>

💡 滚轮缩放 · 拖动平移 · 顶部页签切换 · 下载 .drawio

💡 滚轮缩放 · 拖动平移 · 顶部页签切换 · 下载 .drawio

💡 滚轮缩放 · 拖动平移 · 顶部页签切换 · 下载 .drawio

💡 滚轮缩放 · 拖动平移 · 顶部页签切换 · 下载 .drawio

#### 1c. Architecture comparison — N/A for a benchmark paper

NoLiMa is model-agnostic. The comparable artefacts are *other long-context benchmarks*, summarised in Table 1 above (NoLiMa vs RULER vs BABILong vs ∞Bench vs HELMET) and discussed in Related Work.

2. Scale & Construction (the analogue of "training" for a benchmark paper)

#### 2a. Dataset scale

DimensionValueNotes
Needle templates5 groups (Table 7), each in default + invertedAll listed in Appendix A
Total needle-question pairs58After expanding character / Wq / Wn variants
Books in haystack pool10Open-license, ≥ 50K tokens each
Snippet size in shuffled haystack< 250 tokensRandom concatenation defeats memorisation
Haystack pool size per L5 randomly assembled, each ≥ 60K tokens (≥ 2K lines)Resampling per test
Placements per L26 (default), 51 for smoothed plots, 11 for 64K/128KEqual token-spaced
Tests per L (main table)7,540 = 58 × 26 × 5Per model
Context lengths evaluated250, 500, 1K, 2K, 4K, 8K, 16K, 32K (+ 64K/128K for selected)8 + 2 lengths

#### 2b. Construction recipe (≅ "training data") — itemised

Stage 0: Book corpus selection. 10 open-license English books, each ≥ 50K tokens. Source URLs in data/download_books.sh.

Stage 1: Distractor word filtering (Section 3.1). For every question keyword $W_q$:

1. Embed every word in the book pool with Contriever (Izacard et al. 2022).

2. Compute dot-product similarity to $W_q$.

3. Take top-20 candidates per $W_q$.

4. Author manually reviews; flags substring or high-semantic matches.

5. Remove every sentence containing a flagged word.

Stage 2: Plausible-alternative-answer filtering. Sliding window of 1000 chars / 800-char stride. For each chunk × each question:

1. Prompt Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct with a few-shot template; ask whether any text in the chunk could plausibly answer the question.

2. Output is "N/A" or a candidate span.

3. Author manually reviews each flagged span; redacts or keeps.

4. Loop full pass; stop when no further removals.

Stage 3: Filter validation. Insert each needle into 100 random chunks; re-ask the filter LLM. Result: 99.8% detection rate ⇒ filter LLM has the world knowledge to spot true answers.

Stage 4: Haystack assembly per test. For each test: iteratively pick a random book, extract a continuous snippet (< 250 tokens), append until ≥ 2K lines (≥ 60K tokens). Then truncate to target L.

Stage 5: Needle placement. Insert the chosen needle at the target depth (resolved to the nearest \n token). Optionally insert a distractor sentence subject to the 20% distractor-free zone.

Stage 6: Evaluation. Apply task prompt template (Appendix C); run model with engine-appropriate decoding; score with contains metric.

#### 2c. Compute cost

Not detailed in the paper. Back-of-envelope: 7,540 tests × 8 lengths × 21 main-table models = 1,266,720 inferences, with average context ~10K tokens → on the order of $10K-$30K in API costs for the closed models. Open models on a single 8×H100 node should run in a few wall-clock days for the 70B class, weeks for 405B at 32K.

#### 2d. Construction recipe table

StageGoalInputOutputKey technique
0Source corpusURLs10 books, ~50K+ tok eachOpen-license filter
1Strip distractorsBook + W_q listDistractor-clean bookContriever embed + manual review
2Strip alternative answersDistractor-clean book + questionFinal bookLlama 3.3 70B + manual review (loop)
3Validate filterFinal book + needles99.8% control accuracyInsert-and-detect protocol
4Haystack assemblyFinal books, target LPer-test haystackRandom concatenation, snippet < 250 tok
5Needle insertionHaystack + needle + (depth, distractor)Test promptNewline-aligned token insertion
6EvaluationTest promptPer-test pass/failcontains metric on character name

#### 2e. Construction techniques — itemised

  • Memorisation defence: snippet shuffling within the haystack — even if a model has memorised a book, the random ordering breaks contiguous passages so the irrelevant text is no longer recallable as a unit.
  • Tokenisation bias mitigation: characters drawn from a diverse name pool randomized per test; names already present in the haystack are excluded.
  • Lexical-marker neutralisation: $W_n$ choices avoid orthographic markers that would hint at $W_q$ (e.g., avoid German-spelled cities pointing to Germany).
  • Causal-attention probe: deliberate construction of default vs inverted templates to isolate ordering effects (Section 4.4.1).
  • Position-encoding-vs-attention disambiguation: aligned last-2K placement (Section 4.4.2) — the central methodological contribution that lets you attribute drops to attention load rather than RoPE decay.
  • Direct/MC ablations (Section 4.4.4): operationalise "what is the value of literal matching" as a pp delta against the no-overlap baseline.
  • Distractor injection: standardised templates ("There was an article about $W_q$ in the daily newspaper") with a 20% buffer zone.

#### 2f. Scaling/construction ablations

  • Smoothing: moving-average window 12 over 51 placements (vs raw 26) for cleaner Fig 3 trends; raw plots shown in Appendix G.
  • Reduced placements at 64K/128K: 11 instead of 26 to manage API cost (Appendix E). Adds variance but the authors verify trends are consistent.
  • Token-cap workaround for GPT-4o: 127,500-token haystack cap (since input + output must fit 128K). Same cap applied to Gemini 2.0 Flash for fairness.

#### 2g. Construction barrier (the "hardest to replicate" piece)

The single hardest-to-replicate artifact is the iterative human-in-the-loop alternative-answer filter (Stage 2). It requires (a) an LLM strong enough to act as judge (the paper uses Llama 3.3 70B; the 99.8% control-test number is the calibration), (b) authors with sufficient world knowledge to manually adjudicate flagged spans, and (c) iteration to convergence. Without this step the haystacks would silently leak alternative answers at long L, inflating scores. A naive re-implementation that skips this step and just runs Contriever filtering would likely produce noticeably higher accuracy at 16K-32K.

3. "Quantization & Compression" — N/A

NoLiMa does not study weight quantization, KV-cache quantization, or compression. However: it provides a clean evaluation setup for *quantization regression* (does W4A8 break long-context recall more than short?). Worth following up: run NoLiMa on a quantized Llama 3.3 70B (W4A16 / W4A8 / W4A4) and compare effective lengths.

4. Performance & Evaluation Metrics

  • Base score = max(mean(accuracy(L=250)), mean(accuracy(L=500)), mean(accuracy(L=1K))) per (q, needle), averaged across pairs. Captures how well the model handles the *task* at short context.
  • Effective length = max L such that accuracy(L) ≥ 0.85 × base score. Inspired by Hsieh et al. (RULER) which used Llama 2's 4K performance as the threshold; NoLiMa's per-model relative threshold is more model-agnostic.
  • Normalised score = accuracy(L) / base. Used in plots to make trajectories of differently-strong models visually comparable.
  • Aligned-depth analysis (Section 4.4.2): the methodological contribution that disentangles position-encoding from attention-load.
  • NoLiMa-Hard subset: 10 most-difficult q-needle pairs from the 58, used for reasoning-model comparisons (Table 5; updated in repo for GPT-o3, Gemini 2.5 Pro, etc.).

Cross-benchmark headline: same Llama 3.1 70B has effective length 32K on RULER and 16K on BABILong, but only 2K on NoLiMa. The literal-match shortcut is worth ~16× in effective length for this model.

5. Key Innovations

1. The "minimal lexical overlap" principle for long-context evals. Concretely operationalised as ROUGE-L precision < 0.1 between question and needle. Prior benchmarks ranged 0.5-0.96.

2. Latent-link needle templates with controllable hop count (1 vs 2) and word order (default vs inverted), enabling causal-attention probing.

3. Two-stage haystack filtering pipeline (distractor + alternative-answer) with semi-automatic loop and 99.8%-validated filter LLM.

4. Aligned-depth (last-2K) placement protocol that holds RoPE relative distance constant across L, isolating attention SNR as the explanatory variable.

5. Per-model relative effective length definition (≥ 85% × base score), making comparisons across models with different base accuracies meaningful.

6. Distractor ablation showing that a single $W_q$-containing irrelevant sentence collapses GPT-4o's effective length 8× — the strongest single piece of evidence that LLMs prefer surface matches even when they're content-empty.

6. Infrastructure Impact (cross-layer)

LayerQuestion / Impact
AlgorithmTrain with paraphrased queries / latent-link synthetic data; possibly add NoLiMa-style data to long-context SFT mix. Investigate whether retrieval-augmented attention or fact-store layers (Memory Layers, DSI) help.
KernelNo direct impact — the bottleneck is statistical not computational. But it weakens the quality justification for sparse / sliding-window attention if those further reduce SNR.
FrameworkServing stacks should expose "effective length" as a first-class metadata field alongside "max context length". KV-cache budget planning and autoscaling should target effective-L workloads. RAG retrievers must up-weight semantic relevance over BM25 when feeding low-overlap chunks to the LLM.
AgentTool-use frameworks should keep frequently-needed tool definitions near the query (lexical proximity), or use external retrieval rather than long-context recall for stale items. Recall failures explain anecdotal "agent forgets the tool I gave it 50 turns ago" reports.
HardwareNone directly; long-context HW work (more HBM, smarter attention silicon) does not address the SNR issue.

7. Deployment Considerations

  • For RAG stack owners: any pipeline whose retriever produces low-overlap top-K (e.g., dense retrieval with paraphrased queries) is in the NoLiMa regime — expect substantial accuracy drops vs the BM25 baseline as you grow K.
  • For agent runtimes: structure context so that recall-critical content is either (a) close to the query, (b) lexically tagged with words that appear in the user's last message, or (c) placed in a dedicated retrieval store rather than scratch context.
  • For model selection: do not pick a long-context model based on claimed context length. Use NoLiMa effective length: GPT-4o (8K) > Gemini 2.0 Flash (4K) > Llama 3.3 70B (2K) > Mistral Large 2 (2K) > Claude 3.5 Sonnet (4K). Note that GPT-4.1 has effective length 16K (best in v3 results) and is the current production-friendly leader for genuine long-context tasks.
  • For closed-source picks: GPT-4o and GPT-4.1 are clearly more robust; Gemini 1.5 Pro and Gemini 2.0 Flash are weaker than their billion-token marketing suggests; Claude 3.5 Sonnet is competitive at 4K but degrades sharply past 8K.

8. Position in Model Landscape

NoLiMa positions itself as the "missing rung" between (a) saturated NIAH-style retrieval benchmarks and (b) full long-document QA / book-summarisation that confounds many skills. Combined with RULER (multi-needle, fact-chaining at scale) and BABILong (structured reasoning), it forms a triad: RULER tests *literal retrieval at scale*, BABILong tests *structured task complexity*, NoLiMa tests *associative recall under no surface match*. Together they cover the practical failure surface of long-context LLMs and are likely to coalesce into a standard "long-context regression suite" for new model releases (a role currently occupied informally by RULER + ∞Bench).

The most important downstream signal: the NoLiMa-Hard leaderboard (10 hardest q-needles) is becoming the de-facto reasoning-on-long-context benchmark, with GPT-o3 currently leading at 58.5% / 32K. Future reasoning-model papers will likely report NoLiMa-Hard alongside AIME and MATH.

Open Questions

1. Is the failure attention-mechanism-specific or transformer-specific? Mamba / SSM hybrids (Jamba 1.5 Mini in this paper) show the same pattern, suggesting it might be a property of *recall under low SNR* in any RNN-style sequence model. Test on pure-Mamba and on *encoder-decoder* models to disambiguate.

2. Can long-context training data with NO literal matches close the gap? Train a controlled checkpoint where 50% of long-context training samples have low ROUGE between query and gold span; measure NoLiMa effective length delta.

3. What goes wrong inside the model at the head/layer level? Probe induction heads, function vectors, and key-value attention sparsity as L grows on NoLiMa. Do specific heads "give up" past a threshold L?

4. How much of the GPT-4o advantage comes from training-data composition vs architecture vs RLHF? Without internals, we can only correlate; but the gap suggests OpenAI's long-context post-training pipeline includes something the open community is missing.

5. Does retrieval-augmented attention (e.g. RetNet, REALM-style) close the gap, or is it equivalent to RAG bolted on top? Worth running NoLiMa on retrieval-augmented architectures.

6. Distractor sensitivity vs literal-match training data: is there a training-data signature that explains why GPT-4o degrades from 8K to 1K with one distractor sentence? Could a model be trained to be *robust* to such distractors at the cost of accuracy on literal-match tasks?

Built with Claude Opus 4.6 · Back to index
